items.title: Why acromegaly often requires multimodality therapy
items.text: Microadenoma postoperative remission approaches 90% at expert centers, but macroadenomas—especially with cavernous invasion—remit <50%; combined surgery and somatostatin analogues achieve ~70% biochemical control overall.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "combined use of surgery and somatostatin analogue therapy leads to overall biochemical remission rates of around 70%."

items.title: Why biochemical control of GH/IGF-1 matters for survival
items.text: Acromegaly increases mortality, which can normalize when GH and IGF-1 are controlled—yet long-term morbidity from hypopituitarism, cardiometabolic disease, sleep apnea, arthropathy, fractures, and malignancy persists.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Acromegaly is associated with increased mortality, which can be normalized by control of GH and IGF-1 secretion."

items.title: How to structure long-term acromegaly complication surveillance
items.text: Personalize screening for hypopituitarism, blood pressure, lipids/glucose, sleep apnea, echocardiography, colonoscopy, bone densitometry, spinal and joint imaging, thyroid ultrasound, and quality of life—not all tests repeat at fixed intervals.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "A structured surveillance program must be personalized and should consider: Hypopituitarism Blood pressure Vascular risk factors (lipid profile and glucose intolerance) Obstructive sleep apnea Echocardiography Colonoscopy"

items.title: Why arthropathy, sleep apnea, and quality of life may not improve with biochemical control
items.text: GH/IGF-1 control reduces most complications, but soft-tissue changes and irreversible joint damage often persist—arthropathy correlates with presenting hormone levels.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Exceptions to this rule may be sleep apnea, arthropathy, and quality of life."

items.title: How acromegaly remission affects glucose metabolism
items.text: GH antagonizes insulin action; with biochemical control, insulin resistance improves in almost all patients and diabetes resolves in many after successful surgery.
items.reference: Case 1: "With the fall in GH levels, insulin resistance and glucose tolerance improve in almost all patients, with resolution of diabetes in a number of individuals"

items.title: How to treat pasireotide-induced hyperglycemia
items.text: Pasireotide suppresses insulin and incretin secretion—GLP-1 receptor agonists and DPP-4 inhibitors are most appropriate; sulfonylureas are less ideal.
items.reference: Case 2: "GLP-1 receptor agonists (Answer C) and DPP-4 inhibitors appear to be the most appropriate treatment modality for pasireotide-induced diabetes."

items.title: Two phases of acromegalic arthropathy
items.text: Early phase shows joint space widening from cartilage hypertrophy and ligamentous laxity with some reversibility; later instability leads to narrowing, osteophytes, and cartilage fissuring.
items.reference: Case 3: "The initial phase is characterized by an increase in joint space (Answer A) resulting from hypertrophy of the cartilage and ligamentous laxity."

items.title: When to screen for colonic neoplasia in acromegaly
items.text: Screen at diagnosis and every 5 years until GH/IGF-1 controlled—do not wait until age 50; risk abrogates with biochemical control.
items.reference: Case 4: "At diagnosis and then every 5 years until GH and IGF-1 levels are controlled"

items.title: Complications of acromegaly therapies over time
items.text: Surgery causes early complications; radiation causes hypopituitarism, second malignancies, and vascular disease years later; medical adverse effects add monitoring complexity.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Complications of surgery generally occur early, whereas those associated with radiation therapy (hypopituitarism, second malignancies, vascular disease) usually take a number of years to be realized."

items.title: Why endocrinologists coordinate acromegaly comorbidity management
items.text: After biochemical control, most complications are managed like general-population counterparts, but multidisciplinary care is frequently required.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Management of many of the complications requires multidisciplinary care, frequently coordinated by the endocrinologist."

items.title: Pitfall: expecting sleep apnea to resolve after biochemical remission
items.text: Most studies fail to show resolution of OSA after GH/IGF-1 control—soft-tissue changes often persist.
items.reference: Case 1: "most studies fail to show resolution of sleep apnea with biochemical control of acromegaly."

items.title: Vertebral fracture risk after biochemical control
items.text: Fracture incidence slows with control but remains greater than expected in the general population.
items.reference: Case 1: "The incidence of vertebral fractures (Answer C) does appear to slow with biochemical control; however, the incidence remains greater than expected."

items.question: After transsphenoidal remission of acromegaly, which complication is most likely to improve?
items.explanation: Diabetes improves with falling GH; arthropathy, fractures, and sleep apnea often persist or improve incompletely.
items.reference: Case 1: "thus, Answer A is correct"

items.question: Best medication class for pasireotide-induced diabetes?
options: Metformin alone as sole preferred class
items.explanation: GLP-1 RAs and DPP-4 inhibitors address pasireotide's incretin suppression.
items.reference: Case 2: "GLP-1 receptor agonists (Answer C) and DPP-4 inhibitors appear to be the most appropriate treatment modality"

items.question: Features of acromegalic arthropathy include:
options: All of: widening, narrowing, hyperosteophytes, and cartilage fissuring
items.explanation: All listed features occur at different disease stages.
items.reference: Case 3: "All of these are features of acromegal arthropathy at different stages of the process (Answer E)."

items.question: Recommended colorectal screening in active acromegaly:
options: At diagnosis then every 5 years until GH/IGF-1 controlled
Annual colonoscopy lifelong regardless of control
items.explanation: Polyps and cancers are present at presentation; interval depends on control and prior findings.
items.reference: Case 4: "At diagnosis and then every 5 years until GH and IGF-1 levels are controlled"

items.question: Postoperative remission rate for acromegaly microadenomas at specialist centers is approximately:
items.explanation: Microadenoma remission approaches 90%; macroadenomas fare much worse.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Postoperative remission rates for microadenomas in specialist centers approach 90%."

items.question: Long-acting somatostatin analogues control GH/IGF-1 in what fraction of postoperative nonremitters?
items.explanation: SSAs control axis in 30–40% of surgical failures.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Long-acting somatostatin analogue therapy leads to control of both GH and IGF-in 30% to 40% of patients not in remission following surgery."

items.question: Acromegalic arthropathy progression correlates most closely with:
options: GH and IGF-1 levels at presentation
items.explanation: Arthropathy fails to reverse and correlates with presenting hormone levels.
items.reference: Case 1: "premature degenerative arthropathy (Answer B), which correlates most closely to GH and IGF-1 levels at presentation."

items.question: Regarding malignancy risk in acromegaly:
options: No evidence for any increased colonic polyp prevalence
Increased colonic hyperplastic polyps and malignancies are suggested
Thyroid cancer risk is proven absent
items.explanation: Colonic and thyroid malignancies are most studied; increased polyp/cancer prevalence is suggested.
items.reference: Case 4: "the data suggest there is an increased prevalence of both colonic hyperplastic polyps and malignancies."

items.question: Quality of life in treated acromegaly:
options: May remain impaired despite long-term biochemical stability
items.explanation: QoL is among outcomes with less impact from biochemical control.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Biochemical control has less impact, however, on impaired quality of life and arthropathy"

items.question: Approximate annual incidence of acromegaly is:
items.explanation: Low incidence and nonspecific early features delay diagnosis.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "low incidence (3 to 4 cases/10^6 per year)"

items.question: Approximately what fraction of acromegaly patients still need further therapy after surgery plus SSA?
items.explanation: ~30% require additional therapy beyond surgery + SSA to reach optimal control.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Approximately 30% of patients still require further therapy to achieve optimal disease control."

items.question: Sleep apnea often persists after biochemical control because:
options: Soft-tissue changes from acromegaly frequently remain
items.explanation: Persistent soft-tissue changes explain lack of OSA resolution.
items.reference: Case 1: "the soft-tissue changes observed in patients with acromegaly frequently remain after achieving biochemical control."

items.question: Biochemical control of GH/IGF-1 should be pursued rapidly because:
options: It eliminates all need for surveillance
It reduces severity or prevalence of most complications
It reverses all joint damage within 1 year
items.explanation: Control reduces most complications; exceptions remain for arthropathy, OSA, QoL.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Control of GH and IGF-1 reduces the severity, or prevalence, of most complications observed, and therefore biochemical control should be pursued rapidly and aggressively."

items.statement: Resolution of diabetes occurs in some patients after biochemical control of acromegaly.
items.explanation: Case 1 states diabetes resolves in a number of individuals.
items.reference: Case 1: "with resolution of diabetes in a number of individuals"

items.statement: Biochemical control reliably reverses established acromegalic arthropathy.
items.explanation: Arthropathy progresses despite control and correlates with presenting levels.
items.reference: Case 1: "Biochemical control of acromegaly fails to prevent progression of premature degenerative arthropathy"

items.statement: Colorectal screening in acromegaly should wait until age 50 as in the general population.
items.explanation: Abnormalities are present at presentation—screen at diagnosis.
items.reference: Case 4: "screening should not wait until age 50 years (Answer B), when screening is typically recommended for the general population."

items.statement: Pasireotide inhibits incretin secretion contributing to hyperglycemia.
items.explanation: Mechanism explained in case 2.
items.reference: Case 2: "Pasireotide inhibits not only insulin secretion, but also gut hormones including the incretins."

items.statement: Cavernous sinus invasion lowers postoperative remission rates below 50% even at expert centers.
items.explanation: Significance section states this explicitly.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "remission rates even in specialist centers are less than 50%."

items.statement: Vertebral fracture incidence after biochemical control returns to general-population levels.
items.explanation: Incidence slows but remains greater than expected.
items.reference: Case 1: "the incidence remains greater than expected."

items.statement: Radiation-related hypopituitarism and vascular complications typically manifest years after treatment.
items.explanation: Late sequelae of radiation are emphasized.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "those associated with radiation therapy (hypopituitarism, second malignancies, vascular disease) usually take a number of years to be realized."

items.statement: Control of GH and IGF-1 significantly reduces future colonic polyp risk.
items.explanation: Case 4 states risk is abrogated by biochemical control.
items.reference: Case 4: "Risk also appears to be significantly abrogated by control of GH and IGF-1 secretion"

items.assertion: Assertion: Diabetes mellitus is the complication most likely to improve after acromegaly remission.
items.reason: Reason: GH excess antagonizes insulin action and its reduction improves glucose tolerance.
items.explanation: Both true and mechanistically linked.
items.reference: Case 1: "The excess GH secretion that defines acromegaly antagonizes insulin action, leading to glucose intolerance and diabetes."

items.assertion: Assertion: Sleep apnea often persists after biochemical control of acromegaly.
items.reason: Reason: Most studies show complete resolution of OSA once IGF-1 normalizes.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—resolution is not shown in most studies.
items.reference: Case 1: "most studies fail to show resolution of sleep apnea with biochemical control of acromegaly."

items.assertion: Assertion: GLP-1 receptor agonists are preferred for pasireotide-induced diabetes.
items.reason: Reason: Pasireotide stimulates incretin secretion causing hyperglycemia.
items.explanation: Assertion true; pasireotide inhibits, not stimulates, incretins.
items.reference: Case 2: "Pasireotide inhibits not only insulin secretion, but also gut hormones including the incretins."

items.assertion: Assertion: Early acromegalic arthropathy may show joint space widening.
items.reason: Reason: Widening results from cartilage hypertrophy and ligamentous laxity.
items.explanation: Both true—early reversible phase.
items.reference: Case 3: "an increase in joint space (Answer A) resulting from hypertrophy of the cartilage and ligamentous laxity."

items.assertion: Assertion: Many acromegaly patients require multimodality therapy over time.
items.reason: Reason: Overall postoperative remission rates are 42–65% with most tumors being macroadenomas.
items.explanation: Both true—explains need for combined modalities.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "overall remission rates postoperatively are in the range of 42% to 65%."

items.assertion: Assertion: Colonic screening should begin at acromegaly diagnosis.
items.reason: Reason: Colonic abnormalities are absent until age 50.
items.explanation: Assertion true; abnormalities present at presentation—reason false.
items.reference: Case 4: "These abnormalities are present at presentation"

items.assertion: Assertion: Surveillance regimens in acromegaly should be individualized.
items.reason: Reason: All patients require identical annual testing regardless of remission status.
items.explanation: Assertion true; testing is personalized—not uniform annual repetition.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Individualization of screening is essential"

items.assertion: Assertion: Increased acromegaly mortality can normalize with GH/IGF-1 control.
items.reason: Reason: Mortality remains elevated regardless of any treatment.
items.explanation: Assertion true per source; reason contradicts the text.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "increased mortality, which can be normalized by control of GH and IGF-1 secretion."
